name: plain-writing
description: Write clearly using plain language principles. Reference skill - auto-applied, not invoked directly.

# Plain Writing

Rules for how AI structures its own output.
Applies to summaries, explanations, technical write-ups, and chat responses.

Based on the Federal Plain Language Guidelines, inverted pyramid journalism, and Hemingway's writing principles.

## Sentence Structure

- One idea per sentence.
- Keep sentences under 20 words where possible.
- Put each sentence on its own line within a paragraph.
- Use a blank line to separate distinct topics.
- Use active voice. Say who does what.
- Cut filler words: "basically", "essentially", "in order to", "it should be noted that".

## Document Structure (Inverted Pyramid)

- Lead with the conclusion or most important point.
- Put context and background after the main point, not before.
- Arrange remaining information in descending order of importance.
- The reader should be able to stop reading at any point and still have the key takeaway.

## Word Choice

- Use everyday words. "Use" not "utilise". "Start" not "commence". "About" not "approximately".
- If a technical term is necessary, explain it on first use.
- Avoid nominalisations (verbs turned into nouns). "We decided" not "We made a decision".
- Don't hedge unnecessarily. "This causes X" not "This can potentially cause X".

## Headings and Lists

- Use descriptive headings that tell the reader what they'll learn, not generic labels.
- Use bullet points for 3+ related items.
- Use numbered lists only when order matters.
- Bold the key phrase in each bullet so readers can scan.

## Paragraphs

- One topic per paragraph.
- 2-3 sentences max per paragraph.
- If a paragraph needs more than 3 sentences, break it into two or use a list.

## Explaining Technical Concepts

- Start with what it does, not how it works.
- Use a concrete example before the abstract explanation.
- Compare to something familiar when possible.
- Structure as: **What** (1 sentence) > **Why it matters** (1 sentence) > **How** (details).

## Tables

- Use tables when comparing 2+ things across the same dimensions.
- Use tables for before/after comparisons.
- Keep table cells short. Full sentences don't belong in tables.

## What NOT To Do

- Don't start with background or history. Start with the point.
- Don't use "it is important to note that" or similar throat-clearing.
- Don't write long unbroken walls of text.
- Don't use jargon to sound precise when a simple word works.
- Don't pad responses to seem thorough. Shorter is better if it covers the same ground.

## Sources

These rules are distilled from:
- [Federal Plain Language Guidelines](https://digital.gov/guides/plain-language)
- [NN/g Inverted Pyramid Writing](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/inverted-pyramid/)
- [National Archives Plain Language Principles](https://www.archives.gov/open/plain-writing/10-principles.html)
- Hemingway's writing principles (one idea per sentence, simple words, active voice)
